title: 96-well Growth Curve Analysis using BEAST in R Studio
author: Norman Van Rhijn
license_note: Confirmed via CrossRef DOI metadata: dx.doi.org/10.17504/protocols.io.4fzgtp6
materials: Find the script needed to read your raw data-files. Format the file names like this: plate1-Rep1.txt, plate1-Rep3.txt, plate2-Rep2.txt, plate3-Rep1.txt, plate3-Rep3.txt, plate4-Rep2.txt, plate5-Rep1.txt, plate5-Rep3.txt, plate1-Rep2.txt, plate2-Rep1.txt, plate2-Rep3.txt, plate3-Rep2.txt, plate4-Rep1.txt, plate4-Rep3.txt, plate5-Rep2.txt. Make sure the different replicates are in different files. However, the location in the plate should be exactly the same (i.e., A5 should be A5, B2 should be B2). If this is not the case or replicates are on one plate, transform the original files to match this format. Furthermore, titles of the wells should exactly be the same!

steps.title: Import Data into R Studio
steps.instruction: Import the file from the previous protocol into R studio. R Studio Desktop 1.1.463 The R Studio, Inc. 96-well Plate Growth Curve Setup by Norman Van Rhijn.

steps.title: Preprocess 96-well Growth Data
steps.instruction: Apply the code below. Change the number of reps and plates to your experiment (top two lines). Make sure the data is formatted as described above. The final object returned after applying the code is the list `myDataList`. This preprocessing processes 96-well growth data to match formatting required for the BEAST CRAN package. Different replicates from different files are merged into one dataframe. Apply the provided script.

steps.title: Install BEAST Software
steps.instruction: Install BEAST software from CRAN into R studio. Documentation can be found at: https://rdrr.io/cran/beast/man/beast-package.html and https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/beast/beast.pdf.

steps.title: Run BEAST Analysis
steps.instruction: Run BEAST analysis. Do not run the example included in the package if you want to run your own data. An example dataset (FungalGrowthDataset) is attached to run; if you want an example first, run the top example and then reload your data with the code above. For your own data, only run the code from 'Run MCMC sampler' and 'Print and Plot output lines'. Assume that the observed data consists of N time-series, each with R variables (or replicates) measured at T consecutive time-points. The input of the main function 'beast' should be a list `myDataList` with the following attributes: length(myDataList) should be equal to R (the number of variables or replicates), and dim(myDataList)[[1]],..., dim(myDataList)[[R]] should all be T×N (rows and columns correspond to time-points and different series, respectively). Then, a basic usage of the package consists of the following commands: • beastRun <- beast( myDataList = myDataList ) • print(beastRun) • plot(beastRun). These correspond to running the MCMC sampler, printing, and plotting output summaries. A toy-example is given which reproduces Figure 1 of the paper: library('beast') # load package data("FungalGrowthDataset") # load dataset myIndex <- c(392, 62, 3, 117) # run the sampler only for the # specific subset of time-series set.seed(1) # optional # Run MCMC sampler with the default number of iterations (nIter =70000): run_mcmc <- beast(myDataList = FungalGrowthDataset, subsetIndex = myIndex, zeroNormalization = TRUE) # Print output: print(run_mcmc) # Plot output to file: "beast_plot.pdf" plot(run_mcmc, fileName = "beast_plot_fungal_data.pdf", timeScale=1/6, xlab = "hours", ylab = "growth"). The complexity prior distribution with parameter gammaParameter = 2 is the default prior assumption. Smaller (larger) values of gammaParameter will a-priori support larger (respectively: smaller) number of change-points. Full documentation of the package is given in https://cran.r-project.org/web/packages/beast/beast.pdf.

steps.title: Review Output
steps.instruction: This should result in a PDF file containing all curves combined, clustered in groups with different change-points, and individual curves for each isolate.
